text:
GENITOURINARY MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending Physician]
DIVISION: Genitourinary Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old Polish-American male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, presenting today for his 3-month follow-up visit following initiation of enzalutamide. He comes in for PSA and toxicity assessment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), clinical stage T3bN1M1b, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5 at age 68. His PSA at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 187.4 ng/mL. Staging workup demonstrated osseous metastases at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala on bone scintigraphy, as well as enlarged right external iliac lymph nodes up to 1.8 cm on CT, without visceral metastases. He was initiated on ADT with leuprolide acetate plus abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg twice daily, with bicalutamide flare prophylaxis at ADT initiation. He had an excellent initial response with PSA declining to a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at 12 months. Germline testing performed during the hormone-sensitive phase identified a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) in heterozygous state, confirming hereditary BRCA2 deficiency with implications for PARP inhibitor eligibility.

Approximately one year ago (at approximately 24 months on ADT + abiraterone), Mr. Kowalski demonstrated biochemical and eventual radiographic progression consistent with castration resistance. His PSA had risen to 8.6 ng/mL with a PSA doubling time of approximately 3.5 months, and testosterone confirmed castrate at < 10 ng/dL. Bone scintigraphy at that time was stable/sclerotic, and no visceral disease was identified. He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg orally daily approximately 3 months ago, with continuation of leuprolide acetate to maintain castrate testosterone.

Prior to initiating enzalutamide, a CT-guided biopsy of the L3 vertebral metastasis was performed, and somatic next-generation sequencing (NGS) was obtained. The tumor profile was notable for: BRCA2 biallelic loss (deep deletion with loss of heterozygosity of the germline allele — second-hit mechanism confirmed), TP53 p.R175H (pathogenic hotspot), AR amplification (approximately 8-fold copy number gain), RB1 homozygous deletion, PTEN heterozygous loss, and MYC amplification. Microsatellite status: MSS. TMB: 4 mutations/Mb (low). PD-L1 expression 1% (low). The AR amplification is notable as a potential mechanism of acquired AR-pathway therapy resistance going forward, while the BRCA2 biallelic loss provides a clear actionable target for PARP inhibitor therapy, with olaparib (Lynparza) identified as the next-line option pending current enzalutamide course.

Today, Mr. Kowalski returns 3 months after enzalutamide initiation. He reports overall improvement in his back pain since starting enzalutamide and states he is tolerating the medication reasonably well. He denies any new neurological symptoms. He reports ongoing mild fatigue and hot flashes, which he attributes to his longstanding ADT.

PSA TODAY: 2.3 ng/mL
Prior PSA at enzalutamide start: 8.6 ng/mL
Decline: 73% from baseline

This represents a significant biochemical response to enzalutamide. No imaging has been obtained today per protocol (bone scan and CT deferred to 6-month restaging).

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue, Grade 1. No fever, chills, or night sweats. Appetite stable. Weight stable (no significant loss since last visit). No unintentional weight loss reported.
Musculoskeletal: Low back pain improved, now 1/10 at rest (was 3/10 at enzalutamide initiation). Reports only occasional discomfort with prolonged sitting. No new joint pain. No new bone pain at other sites.
Neurological: No seizures. No syncope or near-syncope. No cognitive changes. No confusion or memory impairment. No falls since last visit. Patient was specifically counseled about seizure risk with enzalutamide at initiation; he has not experienced any events. No paresthesias or limb weakness.
Cardiovascular: Hot flashes, Grade 1-2, pre-existing from ADT, not worsening. No peripheral edema. No chest pain or palpitations. Blood pressure well-controlled per patient report (home readings 120s-130s/70s-80s mmHg).
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation. Appetite normal.
Genitourinary: No hematuria. Urinary frequency/urgency unchanged (mild, chronic from known prostate disease).
Respiratory: No cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Hot flashes as above. No gynecomastia flare (had been noted as mild in past). No symptoms of hypokalemia.
Psychiatric: Mild anxiety regarding disease trajectory, well-managed. Active engagement with family. No depression formally identified.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs:
  Blood Pressure: 128/76 mmHg
  Heart Rate: 68 bpm (regular)
  Respiratory Rate: 16 breaths/min
  Temperature: 98.2°F (36.8°C)
  SpO2: 97% on room air
  Weight: 176 lbs (79.8 kg) — stable from last visit
  Height: 5'10" (178 cm)
  BMI: 25.8

ECOG Performance Status: 1

General: Well-appearing, alert, and oriented x3. Comfortable at rest. No acute distress. Well-nourished male in no apparent distress.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. Mucous membranes moist. No scleral icterus.

Neck: No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains. Thyroid non-palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. Bowel sounds normal. No guarding or rebound.

Lumbar Spine: Mild tenderness to palpation at L3-L4 level, unchanged from prior visit. No paraspinal muscle spasm. No new vertebral point tenderness at other levels.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Full range of motion of bilateral upper and lower extremities.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Reflexes symmetric. Gait steady and unassisted.

Skin: No rash. No palmar erythema. No jaundice.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy (inguinal, axillary, or cervical).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA): 2.3 ng/mL (prior: 8.6 ng/mL at enzalutamide start — 73% decline)
Testosterone: < 10 ng/dL (castrate; confirmed)

Complete Blood Count:
  WBC: 5.8 K/uL (normal)
  Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mild, stable — chronic ADT-related anemia, no change from last CBC)
  Hematocrit: 34.5%
  Platelets: 198 K/uL (normal)
  ANC: 3.2 K/uL (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L (normal; no hypokalemia — abiraterone now discontinued)
  Creatinine: 1.22 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 55 mL/min/1.73 m2 — stable baseline CKD stage 3a)
  BUN: 18 mg/dL
  ALT: 22 U/L (normal)
  AST: 26 U/L (normal)
  Alkaline Phosphatase: 148 U/L (mildly elevated; known to reflect bony disease; stable from prior)
  Total Bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL (normal)
  Albumin: 3.9 g/dL (normal)
  Glucose: 102 mg/dL (borderline; patient counseled regarding ADT-associated metabolic effects)
  Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL (normal)
  Phosphorus: 3.6 mg/dL (normal)
  Magnesium: 2.0 mEq/L (normal)

LDH: 198 U/L (upper limit of normal; non-specifically elevated — worth monitoring)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging performed at this visit per protocol. Restaging bone scintigraphy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis are scheduled at the 6-month mark per standard monitoring intervals for mCRPC on second-line AR-pathway inhibitor therapy. The most recent prior imaging (18 months into ADT + abiraterone, approximately 9 months ago) demonstrated sclerotic changes at the known L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala lesions consistent with treatment response, with right external iliac lymph nodes decreased in size to 0.9 cm, and no visceral metastases at that time.

It is noted that PSMA PET/CT imaging has been discussed but not yet obtained; this will be considered at the time of next restaging, particularly given the BRCA2 biallelic loss and the potential future need for PARP inhibitor therapy and/or other PSMA-directed approaches.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT) and confirmed somatic biallelic BRCA2 loss on NGS of bone metastasis, presenting 3 months after initiation of enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily. He is demonstrating a meaningful PSA response, with PSA declining 73% from 8.6 to 2.3 ng/mL, and reports significant improvement in back pain (1/10 from 3/10 at start). Overall, this is an encouraging early response.

1. mCRPC — Responding to Enzalutamide
   - PSA response is substantial at 73% decline at 3 months. While we use PSA as a surrogate, it is a reasonable indicator of disease activity in this setting.
   - Back pain improvement is clinically consistent with disease response.
   - Testosterone remains castrate — confirming CRPC disease is being adequately suppressed at the gonadal level; enzalutamide is working at the AR signaling level.
   - It is important to contextualize this response in light of the somatic tumor profile: the AR amplification (8x copy number gain) identified on NGS and the prior progression through abiraterone raise concern for durability of response to enzalutamide. However, we will monitor and continue enzalutamide so long as he is benefiting.
   - PARP inhibitor planning: olaparib (Lynparza) remains the clear next-line option given BRCA2 biallelic loss — FDA-approved per the PROfound trial data for mCRPC with HRR gene alterations. We will hold this in reserve for progression.
   - Plan: Continue enzalutamide 160 mg orally daily. Continue leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months for castrate testosterone maintenance (next injection due per schedule).
   - Restaging imaging (bone scan + CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) scheduled at 6-month mark.

2. Bone Metastases / Skeletal Health
   - Known osseous disease at L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala — stable on most recent scan with sclerotic changes.
   - Bone-protective therapy: Patient is not yet on a bone-modifying agent. Given history of bone metastases, mCRPC status, and prolonged ADT use (now > 3 years), initiation of bone-protective therapy should be addressed at or before the next visit. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SQ monthly or zoledronic acid will be discussed at the 6-month visit contingent on renal function (current eGFR ~55 mL/min — borderline for zoledronic acid; denosumab may be preferable).
   - Patient counseled regarding calcium and vitamin D supplementation (calcium 500 mg daily + vitamin D 1000 IU daily) in the interim. He is already taking these per prior recommendation.
   - Dental clearance recommended prior to initiating bisphosphonate/denosumab; patient instructed to follow up with his dentist.

3. Adverse Effects of Enzalutamide
   - Fatigue: Grade 1, well-tolerated. This is expected given both enzalutamide and longstanding ADT. No dose modification warranted at this time. Encouraged light exercise (walking) as tolerated per STAMPEDE data on physical activity in prostate cancer.
   - Hot flashes: Grade 1-2, pre-existing from ADT, not worsening with enzalutamide addition. Venlafaxine was previously discussed and declined by patient; cyproterone not available in U.S.; patient manages with behavioral strategies.
   - Seizure risk: Patient counseled again regarding enzalutamide's seizure risk (estimated < 1% in clinical trials, higher in those with prior seizure history — none in this patient). Instructed to report any unusual movements, loss of consciousness, or confusion immediately. He has not required CNS-active medications that lower seizure threshold.
   - Cognitive effects: Patient endorses no cognitive concerns. Given his age (71) and the theoretical concern for enzalutamide's CNS penetration and cognitive effects in older patients, this will be monitored longitudinally.
   - No hypokalemia now that abiraterone/prednisone is discontinued — electrolytes normal.
   - Mild anemia (Hgb 11.6 g/dL): Chronic, consistent with ADT-related anemia of a patient on long-term hormonal therapy; no change from prior. No intervention required at this time; monitor CBC at next visit.
   - Blood pressure: Well-controlled. Abiraterone-associated hypertension is no longer a concern now that abiraterone is discontinued.

4. Germline BRCA2 — Family Implications
   - Germline BRCA2 c.5946delT (pathogenic) confirmed previously. Patient has been counseled regarding implications for first-degree relatives. Discussed again today — patient reports his son has been referred for genetic testing. Daughter has also been referred (50% risk for BRCA2 carrier status; implications for breast, ovarian, prostate, pancreatic cancer risk).
   - No new germline concerns to address today.

5. Comorbidities / General Health
   - CKD stage 3a (eGFR ~55 mL/min/1.73 m2): Stable. Relevant for future renal-dosing considerations (zoledronic acid threshold, carboplatin dosing if ever needed).
   - Cardiovascular risk: ADT-associated metabolic syndrome risk ongoing (mild hyperglycemia noted today at 102 mg/dL — counseled on diet and activity; will recheck HbA1c at 6-month visit). No formal diabetes diagnosis at this time.
   - Osteoporosis risk: Prolonged ADT now > 3 years. DEXA scan has not been performed; will order at next visit to establish baseline bone density given ADT-related bone loss risk in addition to osseous metastatic disease.

6. Palliative and Supportive Care
   - Patient continues with his primary care physician for management of chronic comorbidities.
   - Nutrition: Weight stable; no registered dietitian referral needed at this time.
   - Psychosocial: Patient is engaged and informed. Family support strong. No formal psychiatric referral at this time. Provided contact information for cancer support resources.
   - Advance care planning: Briefly revisited. Patient has designated a healthcare proxy (his spouse). Discussions will continue to deepen as disease trajectory evolves.

7. Return to Clinic
   - Follow-up in 6 weeks for repeat PSA, CBC, CMP.
   - Full restaging (bone scan, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) at 6 months from enzalutamide start.
   - If PSA continues to rise or clinical symptoms worsen before scheduled imaging, urgent restaging visit to be arranged.
   - Patient instructed to call clinic for any new bone pain, neurological symptoms, dyspnea, hematuria, or confusion.

Time spent: 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of visit time devoted to counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed: [Attending Physician, MD]
GU Medical Oncology, [Institution]
Co-signed: [Fellow/NP, MD/NP, GU Oncology]
